speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, the river I was skating on when I was young, I used to skate from one side of the river to the other in the winter. And I know my father used to go there to bath, because only-- only way when anybody could ever swim is-- was in that river. And then when Bilgoraj burned, it burned down. After this it was in ruins. See, our city was divided in two parts. It was the old part where we lived. And it was the new part, with new buildings, and that was the one which was saved, somehow miraculously wasn't burned. So it took us a day until we arrived with the little bit, what we saved from the fire. We arrived to the other side of the city. And my father had a friend there. So he decided to ask him could we stay with him for a little while, which we did. But after about a week or 10 days, the lady of the house wasn't very happy to have people there. And my father-- my mother felt very uncomfortable. So she decided-- she said-- she said, Dad, look, we have to do something to find some accommodation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went out, and-- and accidentally met the burgermeister, and I can't remember his name. And he said the situation we are in. Would he be-- would he know of anyone who would be willing to rent us some sort of accommodation for the family, for our family. So he says to him, look, don't worry. You can come to stay with me. And he accommodated us. He had an attic, which was a room and a half really. And anyway, we took the little bit of belongings we had, and we moved into his house. When the Germans came into the city, I think it was about the 11th of September, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they decided to have the headquarters, the gestapo decided to have their headquarters in this particular gentleman's house where we were staying. You can imagine. We saw the Germans bathing next to the-- how do you call the-- the water? Oh, god, I can't say it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well?

speaker: interviewee
text: A well, yeah. They were bathing there in front of it. And we were obsessed looking at them. And whether they knew that-- that we are hiding there or not, we don't know up till now. But we-- we stayed there for a few-- for maybe two or three months in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- where did you get food from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. He used to supply us, I think. I really can't really remember how. But I think I was running out sometimes to get a bit of something, run to my grandfather. They-- see, my grandfather and all the family-- the school yard miraculously was saved. And lots and lots of families were living in that school, and my grandfather as well. And we managed to get some food from them. And we used to live on sardines and a bit of bread. Of course we never cooked anything. Because there was no possibility where to cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents ever going out of that attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother went once out, just before we left Bilgoraj. Because my father decided that we are leaving Bilgoraj, but we haven't got any possibility to travel, because-- so he decided that we'll buy a buggy and a horse, and we leave. But that-- that's a story on its own. So what-- what I wanted to say is we lived there for a while. And then my-- they start-- the Germans came in. No, wait a minute. The Germans came in once. And then after 10 days, they left Bilgoraj, and the Russian came in. The Russians stayed with-- in Bilgoraj maybe for another week. And the Germans came in again. And then the problems started. So they start calling the Jews for work. And who didn't register there, they were-- there were in trouble. So my father decided to go to a little township not far from Bilgoraj where my auntie was leaving, and see what's going on there, whether the situation is better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that township?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarnogrod. And he came, after two days he came back. And he said, it's exactly the same story there. They're calling the Jews to work. And I don't want to go to work. So we have to go, and he heard that in Tarnogrod that lots of Jews escaping illegally. There's a border between Poland, which belonged to the Germans, and the Russians, which they divided Poland in-- in the River Bug that was divided Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there is a possibility that we could escape from the Russian-- from the German side. Were you still living with the mayor?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the-- yeah, with the mayor.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Germans underneath you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We lived there all the time until we left the city. And so he said, he'll go by himself and we'll stay where we are. And if there is a possibility to escape, he'll-- he'll come back for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mom says, no way you're going on your own. Either we go all together or we stay all together. So he said, well, he can't help it. So at that time, he sent my mother out. And she bought a horse, and she bought a buggy. And we put whatever we got, and you wouldn't believe it, that the mayor took us out of the city. It was 6:00, 7:00, 5 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whether the Germans saw us or not, but they-- they didn't say anything. And they took us out of the city, and we arrived to a place where another friend of my father was living. He had an estate there. And he promised to give my father another horse and a driver to take us to the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your grandparents, your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, unfortunately, the day before we left, my mom said, you better go and tell my father that we are leaving. So of course, who was the one who was going? Me. So I ran-- I don't know. It didn't took me more than 10 minutes. And it was quite far, because my heart was pumping. And I said, I was frightened. Because it was early in the morning. And I arrived to my grandfather's house. And nobody's-- and my-- my auntie was there. My step-grandmother was there. And she said, your grandfather's already at work. But if you want to catch him, just run out to that and that street. I can't remember the street. You might see him. So I ran again. I arrived there, and the whole-- I don't know how many Jews were there marching. And my grandfather was in the first row. And I saw him and his beard-- he had a nice long beard. [POINTING] Half of the beard was shorn. And he saw me, and I sort of told him, he-- he must have understood that we are going away with my hand. And he just kissed me like that. And said, bye-bye. And that was it. And that was-- you can imagine I was crying very much. Because I knew that was the last time I'll see him. And I never saw him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were they being guarded by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The Germans were guarding. They took them up to-- to build whatever they were building there. And-- and I don't know how long he-- I think that was 1939. I think they perished in '40 or '41. The whole family was gone. Up till now, I don't know where, whether Majdanek or Belzec. But they perished there. And so now the story back to escaping from Bilgoraj. So we-- as I said before, we stayed overnight at this friend of my father's. And the next morning, the driver and another horse, we had now two horses and a buggy. And we all sat on the-- my father was walking. And then-- then after we-- we sat all on the buggy. And we drive, and drove, and drove. And we arrived to the country town where the border was. It was already maybe lunchtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that little town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not remember, not possibly. I can't remember. And there are lots of people-- it was-- I think it must be a Sunday. Because lost of people were on the street. And there's a couple of fellows were standing in a corner. So our driver's name was Stascek. He asked them, where-- because one only Jew was living in that country town. Where does he live? So they looked at each other, and said, oh, you if you go just directly this way, you can't miss him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, of course, he turned and we were going actually in the right direction. But he told him where to go. So he turned the horse and buggy to the left side, and we start continuing to walk. And all of a sudden, I stand up and I said to Daddy, Daddy, tell Stascek to stop the-- we're not going where the-- so he-- so he asked daddy, child, what are you talking about? I said, I don't think so. I think they were smiling to each other. I don't believe they told us the truth. Please tell him to stop and turn the buggy around, which he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually we arrived to the right place. And the Jew who helped us later to go through the border said that God sent an angel in your daughter. Because if you would have gone the direction which these two fellows told you would have never survived. You would have gone straight to the Gestapo hands. Because they were-- they were-- they were sending people there. And we stayed overnight in this place. And the next morning we managed to-- it cost us money. I know my father paid quite a bit of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did he pay the money to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Jew. Because he was-- he was bribing the others. So I don't know how much profit he had. But otherwise you couldn't have possibly go through. And we arrived to a place-- I can't remember the place. I can't remember. It was actually a holiday place my mother used to go before the war. And we stayed there for a little while, not long. And one of our horses died there. [LAUGHS] And we managed to sell the buggy and horse, what we were left with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we traveled. And somebody told us to go to there's a little city. It's called Mizocz. And lots-- a couple of families from our city were already there, and you can settle then. That was already the-- the Russian part, Mizocz belongs to it. Because when the-- when the middle of the war, the-- between the Russians and the Germans, was a pact that they won't-- a pact of no aggression. Right? So, we lived in that little city, I think, about maybe half a year. My father got a job. From-- from what time to what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say, till beginning of '40 middle of '40. My father got a job with one-- one of the firms, a Russian firm really. He was-- an accountant there for a while. And they were very happy with him. And then came a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they decided that all the Jews, the Polish Jews or whoever lived there, should become Russian citizens. And in order, we should take out Russian passports.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before that happened, do you remember what you did for those six months? Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no school that's during the war. We didn't go to school. I can't remember what I was doing. But I don't think that there was any school. We were staying home. I don't know. I really don't remember anything. I really-- it's like a blank. And now-- and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about passports.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And so my father decided he's not going-- he'd rather go back to Bilgoraj then become a Russian citizen. He remembers still before the first World War how problems were with the Russians. So he said, there's no way he's going to become a Russian citizen. And after about a few weeks later, one night, 12 o'clock at night, there's a knock at the door. And two people-- two fellows came in. And said to my father, take your family. Take one suitcase, put whatever you can. We're going away from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father said, what do you mean? I'm working. I've got a job. No. You're coming with us. And they took us to a wagon. And on the station, on the railway station. And there was a couple of other families already there. And my father tried to contact his boss, and they tried to bribe them. He said to give them money, and give them watches, and whatever. Nothing helped. There's no-- at that time it was so politically-- that nothing-- nothing in the world you could have said not to go, not to-- to stay there. So and-- and we stayed there maybe 12 hours on the station. No one could come to say hello to us. And then they hitched [? our ?] wagon to a big train. And we start traveling. And we traveled, and traveled, and traveled. And that was-- the situation was bad. Because we didn't-- you know, the-- the toiletry was no good. And the food wasn't good. We couldn't-- it was really, really very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the stations we looked out, and you wouldn't believe it. The younger brother of my father was standing there waiting to pick up a train. And this came back to me. I forgot to tell you that when you interviewed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father saw him. So he starts screaming, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], what are you doing here? He said, I'm running away from Rovno, and I was going towards Mizocz to see you. And he would have taken another train to come to Mizocz. And it's just an accident we saw him. So he came to join us. And he came with us all the way, because they didn't count how many people were there. As long as they-- they were all there. So one more person wouldn't matter. How many people were on your train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about 20 or 21 or 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know them all?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one family which we knew. It was a dentist, a bachelor, and another family which he later-- the son wrote-- actually he wrote the book he-- on Bilgoraj. He wrote it. And we traveled maybe for three and a half weeks. And all of a sudden we're arriving, and we saw where we are. We are in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us to a displaced-- to a prison camp, which used to be for the Russian prisoners. And they vacated that prison camp for us, for 3,000 Jews. Where did all those Jews come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all different parts of Poland, from-- from Lemberg, Lvov, and Rudna, all this part of Poland, which belongs to Russia. And-- and that-- then this friend of my father said to-- when we arrived to Asino, I can't describe you the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you try?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll try. It was so dirty, full of rats. And it was just-- we actually didn't go to sleep the first night. They fumigated the whole place. And-- and then they gave us a family now of six, because my father's brother was with us, a little tiny room, which we lived there for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks. I don't know how many families were living, but every family was given one room, five people, six, seven people in one room. There was the dining room, the lunch room, this-- everything there, the bedroom, that's it, one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about sanitary facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember where they were. I think we were going outside. There was toilets outside. And can you imagine Siberia, 40 below zero? It wasn't a joke.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Autumn, and we stayed all through the winter. And believe me, I still got a-- a frozen leg from that. It wasn't easy. But being young, I mean, I went to school. And I was happy at school, even if we didn't have much to eat. But I used to walk four kilometers to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did walk outside of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were. We were allowed to go outside of the camp to school, the children were allowed-- allowed to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the-- where the adults allowed outside of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. They had to get special permission. My mother used to go out, and my father used to bring from-- he got straightaway a job. He was managing three restaurants, I mean restaurants-- I wouldn't tell them restaurants. I would tell them more so kitchens for the inmates, us, and then one for the people who are running the-- and for the staff. So-- and he used to calculate the food. I don't know how he did it. Because he never knew to boil an egg before the war. But he managed. And because of that really, we never were hungry. Because he used to bring bread. He used to bring soup. He used to bring whatever he can from the-- the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who is in charge of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians, I mean, there was-- was a boss there. I mean the [INAUDIBLE] there, whatever, you know. And my mother used to go out with bread, whatever she could to that village, where the Russian-- where actually Russians were living, and she used exchange from bread to meat. And she used to bring the meat, and she makes them kosher, un-kosher meat, she used to salt them. You know how kosher meat has been done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Asino.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where the Russians lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians lived too. But it was-- the whole, the whole village was called Asino. But we-- we were living in the camp, which the prisoners of war, or the prisoners of-- Russian prisoners lived before we came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a fence around the area that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards at the gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you wanted to go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had to get special permission to be able to get out of there. And we lived there for quite-- till-- till the war started between Russia and Germany, we lived there. And then after that, we got permission to move out of there. And then my father was even offered to stay on. Because they liked his work that he could work. So he said, no way will I stay here. We'll go further. And we again, bought a wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on to that, can we just go back to Asino? Can you tell me a bit of what your school was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I liked to go to school. Because first of all, I met with lots of people, which not-- not only my friends, but the Russian friends. And I loved to go. Because it-- it was different than to staying-- in the-- in the barracks, which was no life. And I loved school. And I-- I used to participate in school activities. And-- and so for me, school was very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish communal life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any celebration--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little in the barracks. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any celebration of Jewish holidays? Were you aware of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware of them. I remember Pesach. My father used to even to bake matzos in the little stove what we had. He used to make his own matzos. And we used to celebrate whatever we could. But we could not really keep it 100% kosher. It was not possible. We were very lucky that we could do what we did. Because it's-- what can I say? It wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russians do anything when-- when you baked matzos or when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not know. I mean we did it in our own quarters. So they didn't know. They did not came to our rooms and see what we are doing. But we couldn't do many things what you wanted to do, put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there anybody who-- who did carry on their religious lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody, I mean, you know, the Polish Jews, most of them were religious. You know, there was quite a few which were not. But most of them were religious, whoever could. I know there was a family friends of our parents who came also together in the same wagon with us. And he was very-- much more religious even than we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my father used to calculate the-- the menu of the kitchen, and sometimes he used to really-- nothing was there which could be not kosher. So he used to tell his friend, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], I think it was his name. Now, you can come to take something to eat. Because, you know, it is kosher, whatever you think kosher is. I mean we were happy that we had something to eat. You see? So but-- I really-- it's so far away. I'm-- I'm lucky that I remember that. I can tell you this. What was your status? Were you prisoners of war or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah-- yes, yes. I must say so. Yes, we were. Because we didn't have all the rights. We only got the rights after when they-- when the pact of-- when the war started between Germany and Russia. And then they freed us. Right? Then they told us you can go wherever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that, in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41. And so we decided, our family, I think 21 people, we-- we bought a wagon. We actually had to pay for it. And that wagon had-- had-- it was attached to the railway. And we were traveling with big carriages. Our carriage was attached to other carriages. We would travel for nearly four weeks all through, from Siberia to Middle Asia, to Uzbekistan, and stopped on different stations. We used to pick up food on the stations. And then we arrived to Tashkent. And we stayed overnight there. And from Tashkent, we go further. And someone was telling us, my-- my parents that there's a little city called Leninsk. There's lots of Jews, Polish Jews, are living there. So maybe we-- we try to settle there. And then we arrived to Leninsk, some people came to actually meet us. I don't know how they knew that anybody is coming. Because there's no-- there was no communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they start talking to my father. And they said, look, why don't you come and stay here? You can find here, you know, we help you find you accommodation. And so we took everything out of the wagon. And we decided to live there. And I will never forget it. Then they took us to a place that was the little house built of clay. There was no windows, no doors, no beds. And it belonged to the Uzbeks that house. And they brought us some mattresses out of straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how it happened that my father and my uncle used-- they made the windows from what-- they got some wood. And they put some windows together. I mean there was still the shutters. But it was not really actually windows and doors. And we stayed there, two families in two rooms. We had one room, and the other family of four people, six people, they had also a mother with them that stayed with them. And we lived there for nearly a year, and then we managed to get another house where we stayed till year 1945, until we left Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break there for the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
